content.text: A vendor scoring framework provides a structured, repeatable methodology for evaluating and comparing AI model providers. Without a formal scoring system, vendor decisions default to recency bias (whoever demoed last), price anchoring (cheapest option without TCO analysis), or capability theater (benchmark results that do not reflect production conditions). A well-designed scoring framework makes the evaluation criteria explicit, the weighting defensible, and the decision auditable.
What It Is

A vendor scoring framework is a weighted multi-criteria evaluation instrument that systematically scores competing AI vendors across standardized dimensions: technical capability, security and compliance, commercial terms, and ecosystem factors. Each dimension decomposes into concrete sub-criteria with defined scoring rubrics, allowing evaluators to produce comparable numerical scores that support both team deliberation and executive decision documentation.

Unlike informal capability comparisons, a scoring framework decouples 'what matters' (weight assignment, decided before evaluation) from 'how well does this vendor do it' (scoring, done during evaluation). This separation prevents confirmation bias where evaluators unconsciously inflate scores on dimensions where a preferred vendor happens to be strong. The framework also creates a reusable artifact: once weights are agreed on for a class of use case (customer-facing chatbot, internal copilot, batch document processing), future vendor evaluations reuse the same framework with only the scoring updated.
Why It Matters

AI vendor selection decisions carry multi-year lock-in risk, six-to-seven-figure cost implications, and direct regulatory exposure. A scoring framework is not bureaucratic overhead — it is the documented evidence that due diligence was performed. When a model vendor deprecates an API, when a security incident surfaces, or when contract renewal negotiations begin, the original scoring documentation provides the baseline for understanding whether the vendor still meets organizational requirements.
Prevents anchoring bias: separating weight decisions from scoring decisions stops pre-selected vendors from dominating
Enables comparison: normalized scores allow side-by-side comparison of vendors with very different capability profiles
Creates accountability: documented criteria and scores make vendor selection rationale defensible to auditors and executives
Surfaces hidden risks: structured security and compliance dimensions force evaluation of factors often skipped in informal evaluations
Supports re-evaluation: documented baseline scores allow structured re-evaluation when vendor circumstances change
Aligns stakeholders: multi-dimensional scoring engages security, legal, finance, and engineering in structured dialogue rather than competing advocacy
Reduces political capture: numerical scores create a shared fact base that reduces pure politics in vendor decisions
Enables portfolio management: consistent frameworks allow comparison across multiple concurrent vendor evaluations
Benefits
Reproducible evaluation process that can be executed by different team members with consistent results
Explicit trade-off documentation: a vendor may score higher on technical capability but lower on commercial flexibility, and the framework makes that trade-off visible
Stakeholder alignment: structured scoring sessions surface disagreements about priorities before vendor commitment
Regulatory defensibility: documented evaluation process supports AI governance audit requirements
Negotiation leverage: published scoring criteria communicate to vendors which dimensions are weighted highest, incentivizing them to improve where it matters
Historical record: archived evaluation documents support organizational learning across vendor generations
Scalability: reusable framework enables consistent evaluation across multiple vendors simultaneously
Risk quantification: scoring dimensions explicitly include risk factors (vendor stability, lock-in exposure, compliance gaps)
Trade-offs

Scoring frameworks introduce rigor but also overhead. A rigorous evaluation with POC testing, reference calls, and security reviews takes 4-8 weeks and involves multiple stakeholders. The framework must be calibrated for the decision at stake: a $50k/year API integration does not warrant the same evaluation depth as a $5M enterprise model platform contract.
Weight subjectivity: weight assignments embed organizational priorities that are themselves contested — get stakeholder buy-in before evaluation starts, not after
Benchmark gaming: vendors optimize their products for publicly known benchmarks; require evaluation on organization-specific tasks
False precision: numerical scores imply more accuracy than the underlying criteria support — use score ranges and confidence intervals, not single-decimal scores
Evaluation fatigue: scoring 8+ vendors across 20+ criteria exhausts evaluators; limit initial scoring to top 3-5 vendors after a lightweight triage round
Static snapshots: vendor capabilities change rapidly in AI; re-evaluation triggers (major release, pricing change, security incident) must be defined explicitly
Cost of evaluation: comprehensive POC evaluation costs 200-400 engineer-hours; ensure decision stakes justify the investment
How It Works

The framework operates in three phases. In the weight-setting phase, stakeholders from engineering, security, legal, and finance agree on the relative importance of each evaluation dimension before seeing vendor-specific data. This prevents post-hoc rationalization of pre-made decisions. Weights are normalized to sum to 100%.

In the scoring phase, evaluators independently score each vendor on each criterion using a defined rubric (typically 1-5 or 1-10 scale with anchors at each point). Scoring is done independently before group discussion to prevent anchoring. Group discussion reconciles large scoring disagreements through structured dialogue.

In the synthesis phase, weighted scores are summed to produce a composite vendor score. The framework surfaces not just the winner but the margin, the dimensions where each vendor leads, and the criteria where the top vendors are statistically indistinguishable — critical input for negotiation strategy.
Architecture and Design

attrs.alt: AI vendor scoring framework weighted evaluation model

content.text: The scoring framework decomposes into four weighted dimensions: Technical Capability (25%), Security and Compliance (20%), Commercial Terms (20%), and Ecosystem and Partnerships (35%). The ecosystem dimension receives the highest weight because technical capabilities among top-tier AI vendors have converged significantly, while ecosystem lock-in, roadmap alignment, and support quality remain highly differentiated and have long-tail impact on total cost of ownership.
When to Use It
Initial platform selection: choosing a primary LLM provider for a new AI product or platform
Annual vendor reviews: formal re-evaluation of current vendors against the market at contract renewal
Competitive displacement evaluation: assessing whether a new entrant justifies migration from an incumbent
Multi-vendor architecture decisions: determining which vendors play which roles in a hybrid-vendor portfolio
Make-vs-buy decisions: scoring open-source self-hosting options against managed API providers
Regulatory audit preparation: documenting AI vendor selection due diligence for GDPR, CCPA, or sector-specific regulators
Post-incident evaluation: re-scoring an incumbent after a reliability or security incident to determine whether to continue the relationship
Budget cycle planning: generating objective data to support AI vendor budget requests and trade-off discussions
How to Implement
The following Python implementation models a complete vendor scoring framework with criteria management, independent scoring collection, disagreement detection, and weighted composite scoring.
The following YAML configuration defines the criteria for an enterprise LLM API evaluation, with weights that sum to 1.0 within each dimension.

# vendor-scoring-criteria.yaml — LLM API evaluation framework
dimensions:
  technical:
    weight: 0.25
    criteria:
      model_quality:
        weight: 0.30
        description: "Quality on organization-specific task evaluations (not just public benchmarks)"
        rubric:
          1: "Fails on >50% of internal test cases"
          3: "Meets requirements on 70-85% of test cases"
          5: "Exceeds requirements on >95% of test cases"
      api_reliability:
        weight: 0.25
        description: "Historical uptime, incident frequency, and recovery time"
        rubric:
          1: "<99.5% uptime, >2 incidents per quarter"
          3: "99.9% uptime, <4 incidents per year"
          5: "99.99% uptime, transparent status page, automated failover"
      latency:
        weight: 0.25
        description: "P95 latency for typical request sizes under production load"
        rubric:
          1: ">5s P95 latency"
          3: "1-2s P95 latency"
          5: "<500ms P95 latency with streaming"
      fine_tuning_rag:
        weight: 0.20
        description: "Support for fine-tuning, RAG integration, and tool use"
        rubric:
          1: "No fine-tuning or tool use support"
          3: "Basic fine-tuning, function calling"
          5: "Full fine-tuning pipeline, RAG-optimized embeddings, advanced tool orchestration"

  security:
    weight: 0.20
    criteria:
      data_residency:
        weight: 0.30
        description: "Control over geographic data processing and storage locations"
        rubric:
          1: "No residency controls, data may leave jurisdiction"
          3: "Regional deployment option available"
          5: "Customer-controlled residency with cryptographic attestation"
      certifications:
        weight: 0.30
        description: "SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and sector-specific certifications"
        rubric:
          1: "No independent certifications"
          3: "SOC 2 Type II only"
          5: "SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + sector-specific (HIPAA BAA, PCI DSS)"
      zero_retention:
        weight: 0.20
        description: "Option for zero data retention (inputs/outputs not stored by vendor)"
        rubric:
          1: "No zero-retention option"
          3: "Zero-retention available on enterprise tier"
          5: "Zero-retention default, with cryptographic proof available"
      audit_logging:
        weight: 0.20
        description: "Comprehensive audit logs exportable to customer SIEM"
        rubric:
          1: "Minimal logging, no export"
          3: "Basic request/response logs, CSV export"
          5: "Full structured audit trail, real-time SIEM integration"

  commercial:
    weight: 0.20
    criteria:
      pricing_model:
        weight: 0.35
        description: "Pricing predictability and alignment with usage patterns"
        rubric:
          1: "Opaque pricing, no volume discounts"
          3: "Published token pricing with volume tiers"
          5: "Committed use discounts, reserved capacity, predictable unit economics"
      contract_flexibility:
        weight: 0.35
        description: "Ability to adjust terms, data processing agreements, and exit clauses"
        rubric:
          1: "Standard terms only, no negotiation"
          3: "Enterprise agreement with limited customization"
          5: "Fully negotiable terms, portable data guarantees, exit assistance"
      rate_limits:
        weight: 0.30
        description: "Rate limit generosity and burst capacity for production workloads"
        rubric:
          1: "Restrictive limits that require architectural workarounds"
          3: "Adequate limits for current scale, upgrade path available"
          5: "Generous limits with dedicated capacity option"

  ecosystem:
    weight: 0.35
    criteria:
      roadmap_alignment:
        weight: 0.30
        description: "Alignment of vendor roadmap with organizational AI strategy"
        rubric:
          1: "Roadmap unknown or misaligned with our priorities"
          3: "Roadmap shared; 60-70% alignment with our priorities"
          5: "Strategic roadmap co-development, early access program"
      support_quality:
        weight: 0.25
        description: "Enterprise support responsiveness and technical depth"
        rubric:
          1: "Community-only support"
          3: "Business hours email support, 4h response for P1"
          5: "24/7 dedicated TAM, 15min response for P1, proactive monitoring"
      integrations:
        weight: 0.25
        description: "Native integrations with our existing toolchain"
        rubric:
          1: "API only, no native integrations"
          3: "LangChain/LlamaIndex support, basic observability"
          5: "Native integrations with LangChain, Weights & Biases, Datadog, SIEM"
      vendor_stability:
        weight: 0.20
        description: "Financial stability, funding runway, and organizational maturity"
        rubric:
          1: "Early-stage startup, limited runway"
          3: "Series B+, 18+ months runway, growing team"
          5: "Public company or cash-flow positive, 5+ years history, enterprise references"
Examples
The following code shows how to load a scoring session, compute results, and produce a ranked comparison report:

# Example: running a 3-vendor scoring session
from vendor_scoring import VendorScoringFramework, ScoringCriterion, VendorScore

criteria = [
    ScoringCriterion("model_quality", "Model Quality", "technical", 0.30, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("api_reliability", "API Reliability", "technical", 0.25, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("latency", "Latency P95", "technical", 0.25, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("fine_tuning_rag", "Fine-tuning & RAG", "technical", 0.20, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("data_residency", "Data Residency", "security", 0.30, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("certifications", "Certifications", "security", 0.30, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("zero_retention", "Zero Retention", "security", 0.20, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("audit_logging", "Audit Logging", "security", 0.20, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("pricing_model", "Pricing Model", "commercial", 0.35, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("contract_flexibility", "Contract Flexibility", "commercial", 0.35, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("rate_limits", "Rate Limits", "commercial", 0.30, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("roadmap_alignment", "Roadmap Alignment", "ecosystem", 0.30, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("support_quality", "Support Quality", "ecosystem", 0.25, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("integrations", "Integrations", "ecosystem", 0.25, "...", {}),
    ScoringCriterion("vendor_stability", "Vendor Stability", "ecosystem", 0.20, "...", {}),
]

framework = VendorScoringFramework(criteria)

# Scores from three independent evaluators
raw_scores = [
    # vendor_openai
    VendorScore("openai", "model_quality", "alice", 5),
    VendorScore("openai", "api_reliability", "alice", 4),
    VendorScore("openai", "latency", "alice", 4),
    VendorScore("openai", "fine_tuning_rag", "alice", 5),
    VendorScore("openai", "data_residency", "alice", 3),
    VendorScore("openai", "certifications", "alice", 4),
    VendorScore("openai", "zero_retention", "alice", 3),
    VendorScore("openai", "audit_logging", "alice", 4),
    VendorScore("openai", "pricing_model", "alice", 3),
    VendorScore("openai", "contract_flexibility", "alice", 3),
    VendorScore("openai", "rate_limits", "alice", 4),
    VendorScore("openai", "roadmap_alignment", "alice", 5),
    VendorScore("openai", "support_quality", "alice", 4),
    VendorScore("openai", "integrations", "alice", 5),
    VendorScore("openai", "vendor_stability", "alice", 5),
    # ... additional evaluators and vendors omitted for brevity
]

results = framework.score_vendors(raw_scores)
for vendor_id, rank_score in framework.ranking(results):
    r = results[vendor_id]
    print(f"{vendor_id}: {r.composite_score:.2f}")
    print(f"  Technical: {r.dimension_scores['technical']:.2f}")
    print(f"  Security:  {r.dimension_scores['security']:.2f}")
    print(f"  Commercial:{r.dimension_scores['commercial']:.2f}")
    print(f"  Ecosystem: {r.dimension_scores['ecosystem']:.2f}")
    if r.disagreements:
        print(f"  Disagreements: {', '.join(r.disagreements)}")
Real-World Example

A mid-size healthcare technology company needed to select a primary LLM API provider for a clinical documentation assistant serving 200 hospitals. The vendor shortlist was OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Vertex AI, and Azure OpenAI Service. Given the healthcare context, the security and compliance dimension was re-weighted to 35% (from the default 20%), with the technical dimension reduced to 20%, and the ecosystem weight held at 35%.

The evaluation team ran independent scoring across 5 evaluators (two from engineering, one from security, one from legal, one from product). Initial scoring disagreements were highest on 'roadmap alignment' (stddev 1.8) because engineering and product had different views of the vendor relationship they needed. A structured discussion surfaced that engineering prioritized API stability and fine-tuning while product prioritized consumer-grade UX improvements — two different roadmap concerns that required different vendor strategies.

The final scores showed Azure OpenAI Service winning on security and compliance (HIPAA BAA availability, existing Microsoft enterprise agreement, data residency in US-only regions) while lagging on ecosystem and roadmap alignment. After the scoring session, the team negotiated specific contractual commitments from Microsoft to close the roadmap gap, resulting in a vendor selection they could defend to their compliance auditors and board.
Common Mistakes
Setting weights after seeing scores: weight assignment must happen before any vendor-specific data is reviewed to prevent reverse-engineered justification
Using only public benchmarks: evaluating vendors on MMLU or HumanEval rather than organization-specific test cases produces results that do not predict production performance
Skipping security criteria: 'we can add security later' leads to post-selection discoveries of data residency requirements that block deployment
Single evaluator: individual evaluators have biases; require at least three evaluators from different functions and aggregate scores before discussion
Ignoring vendor stability: a startup with better technology but precarious funding may be outscored by a mature vendor with slightly inferior technology but far lower lock-in risk
One-time evaluation: vendor landscapes change quarterly; establish re-evaluation triggers (major release, pricing change, security incident) as part of the framework
Not including TCO: evaluating API pricing without infrastructure, integration, and support costs produces systematically misleading cost comparisons

Conflating scoring with selection: the framework informs the decision; it does not make it — strategic factors (existing relationships, regulatory preferences) can legitimately override the composite score if documented explicitly
Scope creep: trying to score every possible criterion leads to evaluator fatigue and lower-quality scores on each criterion; limit to 15-20 criteria maximum
Best Practices
Lock weights before the evaluation begins, with sign-off from all key stakeholders; publish the weight document with a timestamp
Require all evaluators to score independently before any group discussion to prevent anchoring
Flag criteria with high evaluator disagreement (stddev > 1.5 on a 5-point scale) for structured group discussion before accepting the score
Include at least one security engineer in every vendor evaluation, regardless of how technical the decision appears
Run a lightweight triage round (8-10 criteria, no POC) to reduce the longlist to 3-5 before investing in a full evaluation
Document the rationale for final selection separately from the score, including any override factors applied after scoring
Archive the complete evaluation package (criteria, weights, individual scores, group discussion notes, final decision) for regulatory and audit purposes
Re-evaluate the incumbent vendor using the same framework at each contract renewal to ensure objectivity
Calibrate evaluator scores with a reference vendor at the start of each session to ensure consistent rubric interpretation
Publish the framework template (without scores) to participating vendors so they understand evaluation criteria and can prepare relevant documentation
Performance and Scalability

For organizations evaluating multiple vendors simultaneously or running annual reviews across a large AI portfolio, the scoring framework must scale efficiently. Key scalability considerations include evaluator time management (limiting the full framework to 3-5 vendors, with a lightweight triage pass for the full market), tooling integration (structured scoring in spreadsheets or purpose-built vendor management tools), and framework maintenance (updating criteria annually to reflect market evolution).
Use a two-stage process: lightweight 10-criteria triage for all candidates, full 20-criteria evaluation for top 3-5
Automate data collection where possible: SLA records, uptime history, certification status, and pricing can be auto-populated from vendor portals
Store scoring artifacts in a centralized repository (Confluence, Notion, SharePoint) with version control for audit purposes
Build a vendor scoring database that accumulates historical evaluation data, enabling trend analysis across evaluation cycles
Template the framework for different use case classes: evaluation criteria and weights differ between customer-facing LLM APIs, batch document processing, and embedded ML APIs
Tooling and Ecosystem
Procurement platforms: Vendr, Zip, Coupa — structured vendor intake and evaluation workflow management
Spreadsheet tools: Google Sheets, Airtable — weighted scoring matrices with evaluator views and aggregation formulas
Vendor management: Sastrify, Zylo — SaaS contract management with renewal triggers and benchmark pricing data
Security assessment: SecurityScorecard, BitSight — automated security posture scoring that feeds into the security dimension
Legal review: Ironclad, Docusign CLM — contract redlining and obligation tracking integrated with vendor selection
Reference checking: Gartner Peer Insights, G2 — structured reference data to validate vendor claims during evaluation
AI-specific benchmarks: HELM, LMSYS Chatbot Arena, OpenLLM Leaderboard — supplementary data for the technical dimension

Security and Reliability
Store scoring documents with access controls — they contain competitive intelligence about organizational priorities that vendors should not see
Include a 'disqualifying criteria' layer: certain security or compliance gaps (no SOC 2, no DPA, no data residency) should disqualify vendors before scoring begins
Validate vendor security claims with independent evidence: request audit reports directly, not through the sales team
Build in a security veto: allow the CISO or head of security to reject a vendor despite a high composite score if a security risk is deemed unacceptable
Document the residual risk accepted when selecting a vendor that scored below threshold on any security criterion
Establish contractual requirements as a prerequisite: vendors must sign a DPA and confirm GDPR/CCPA compliance before entering the evaluation process
Mental Model

Think of a vendor scoring framework as a structured debate format — not a calculator. The numerical scores give the debate a shared factual foundation, but the real value is in the disagreements the framework surfaces. When evaluator A scores vendor X a 5 on roadmap alignment and evaluator B scores it a 2, that discrepancy is not a problem to average away; it is the most important conversation you need to have before signing a multi-year contract.

The framework is also a forcing function for stakeholder alignment. Organizations that use informal vendor selection often discover after the fact that security thought they were selecting for compliance, engineering thought they were selecting for capability, and finance thought they were selecting for cost. The scoring framework makes these competing priorities explicit before commitment, not after.
Key Takeaways
A vendor scoring framework decouples weight-setting (what matters) from scoring (how well does this vendor do it) to prevent reverse-engineered vendor justifications

Standard dimensions for AI vendor evaluation: Technical Capability (25%), Security and Compliance (20%), Commercial Terms (20%), Ecosystem (35%) — but adjust weights for your specific use case and risk profile
Require independent scoring from at least three evaluators representing engineering, security, and business functions before any group discussion
Flag high-variance scoring criteria for structured discussion — they indicate stakeholder disagreement about priorities that must be resolved before selection
Use a two-stage process: lightweight triage to reduce candidates to 3-5, then full evaluation to select
Archive the complete evaluation package for regulatory audit purposes, including the rationale for any composite-score overrides
Establish disqualifying criteria (SOC 2 absence, no DPA availability, unsupported data residency) that eliminate vendors before scoring begins
Re-evaluate the incumbent vendor using the same framework at each contract renewal — organizational requirements evolve and markets shift
The framework informs the decision; it does not make it — document explicitly any strategic factors that override the composite score
Vendor scoring is a reusable organizational capability: invest in maintaining and improving the framework across evaluation cycles
